title: Candle Wax on Linen
timeEstimate: 20–40 minutes; freeze + scrape + iron-through-paper (linen tolerates careful heat)
stainChemistry: Candle wax is paraffin (petroleum hydrocarbon) or beeswax/soy wax + sometimes fragrance + sometimes dye. On linen (cellulose, similar to cotton), the iron-through-paper trick that works on cotton ALSO works on linen — careful low heat through 3–4 paper towels with a brown paper bag transfers the wax up into the absorbent paper. Linen tolerates this better than synthetic carpet (which would melt). Two cautions: (1) structured / lined / dry-clean-only linen = pro from minute one (heat + structure = warping), (2) colored wax leaves a separate dye stain after the wax is gone — treat as dye.
whyThisWorks: Freeze + scrape + iron-through-paper transfers paraffin wax cleanly off linen. Linen is heat-tolerant (vs synthetics) AND iron-friendly when supported with absorbent paper. Goo Gone for residual wax. Separate dye treatment for colored wax. Standard protocol works on simple flat linen.
defaultAssumption: Simple flat linen (napkin, basic tablecloth, plain shirt) = home protocol. Structured / lined / dry-clean-only / vintage = pro. Colored candle wax = treat dye separately after wax is fully gone.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Good light, ice in plastic bag
spottingProtocol.instruction: Dry-clean-only / structured / vintage = pro. Simple flat linen = proceed. Place sealed ice bag on wax 10–15 min.
Crack frozen wax with the edge of a plastic card. Scrape off bulk pieces. Don't gouge linen — flat-edge lift only.

spottingProtocol.equipment: 3–4 paper towels or brown paper bag, iron on LOW
spottingProtocol.instruction: Stack 3–4 paper towels (or a brown paper bag) ON TOP of the wax spot. Iron on LOW for 5–10 sec at a time. Wax wicks UP into the paper. Replace paper as it absorbs. STOP if linen feels hot — linen tolerates careful heat but not extended.
If thin wax film remains after iron-through-paper, Goo Gone on a white cloth tamps the residue. Ventilate. Hidden test on dyed linen first.

spottingProtocol.agent: 3% H₂O₂ for Colored Wax Dye Residue
spottingProtocol.instruction: If wax was colored and dye remains after wax is gone: 3% peroxide damp cloth 5 min. Hidden test on dyed linen.
Cool wash per care label. AIR-DRY FLAT — inspect before any iron / dryer.

steps: 2. Ice freeze 10–15 min. Crack and scrape bulk.
3. Iron-through-paper transfer, low heat, 5–10 sec passes.
4. Goo Gone on cloth for residual wax film.
5. 3% H₂O₂ for residual colored-wax dye (hidden test).
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold (freeze) + low heat for iron transfer + cool wash
warnings: Iron on LOW, 5–10 sec passes only — linen tolerates careful heat but not extended
Replace paper as it absorbs — saturated paper drips wax back
Hidden test before peroxide on dyed linen
Flammable solvent for residual film — ventilate
Pigment is a separate stain after wax is gone

homeSolutions.instruction: Linen is one of the few fibers where the classic "iron + brown paper bag" wax trick works safely. Linen is heat-tolerant (unlike synthetic carpet which would melt). Two rules: (1) structured / lined / dry-clean-only linen = pro from minute one, and (2) colored wax leaves a separate dye stain after the wax is gone — treat that as a dye stain after.
Press a zip-lock bag of ice on the wax for 10–15 minutes. NEVER direct ice on linen. The wax will get hard and brittle.

homeSolutions.agent: Crack and Scrape Bulk — Plastic Card
homeSolutions.instruction: Once frozen, crack the wax with the edge of a plastic card. Lift off bulk pieces. Most surface wax comes off this way.

Stack 3–4 paper towels (or a brown paper bag, no print) ON TOP of the wax spot. Set the iron to LOW. Press for 5 to 10 seconds at a time, NOT longer. Wax wicks UP into the absorbent paper. Replace paper as it picks up wax. Repeat until no more wax transfers. STOP if the linen feels hot under the iron — linen tolerates careful heat but extended heat scorches.

homeSolutions.agent: Goo Gone for Residual Wax Film
homeSolutions.instruction: If a thin wax film remains, dab Goo Gone (orange bottle, citrus solvent) on a clean white cloth and press the residue. Open windows — Goo Gone is flammable. Hidden test on dyed linen first.

homeSolutions.agent: If Colored Candle — Treat the Dye
homeSolutions.instruction: Once the wax is fully gone, you might see the candle's color (red, blue, etc.) still in the linen. That's now a dye stain. Hidden-area test 3% peroxide on white linen 5 min. Skip on dyed linen.
Cool wash per care label. AIR-DRY FLAT. Inspect before any iron or dryer.

steps: 2. Ice freeze 10–15 min. Crack and scrape bulk.
4. Goo Gone on cloth for residual wax film.
5. 3% peroxide for colored-wax dye (white linen only, hidden test).
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if linen is structured / lined / dry-clean-only / vintage. Stop if iron starts to scorch the fiber.
warnings: Iron on LOW only, 5–10 sec passes — extended heat scorches linen
Replace paper as it absorbs — saturated paper drips wax back
Hidden test on dyed linen before peroxide
Pigment is a SEPARATE stain after wax is fully gone
Flammable solvent — no flame near Goo Gone

neverDo: Never extended iron heat on linen — scorches
Never iron on structured / lined linen
fiberSensitivities: Linen tolerates LOW careful iron heat with paper barrier

materialWarnings: Linen is one of the few fibers where iron-through-paper works
LOW heat, short passes — extended heat scorches
Pigment is a separate stain after wax

escalation.when: Structured / lined / vintage linen, dry-clean-only label, iron scorched fiber, residual colored wax dye after peroxide, polymerized wax from being walked on/sat on for weeks.
escalation.whatToTell: "Candle wax on [simple / structured / vintage] linen, [white / colored wax], age [fresh / aged], home-treated with freeze + scrape + iron-through-paper + Goo Gone + [3% peroxide on white]. Cool wash + air-dry flat. [Residual wax / pigment / scorch]."

deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Colored candle wax leaves dye after wax — separate treatment. Vintage linen needs cooler everything; pro for any wax stain. Synthetic-blend linen (linen-cotton blend) follows linen rules; linen-rayon blend = pro (rayon doesn't tolerate heat).
